title: EdgeRelations
lean_code_with_comments:
/-- `v₁` and `v₂` are *adjacent in `G`*: there is some edge of any
orientation between them. Definitionally `sus G v₁ v₂` from
def_3_2; the readable name exists so downstream prose statements
such as claim 3.1's "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" can be cited
without unfolding to `sus`. -/
def Adjacent (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := sus G v₁ v₂

/-- `EdgeInto G v₁ v₂` -- the edge between `v₁` and `v₂` is *into
`v₁`*, i.e. has an arrowhead at `v₁` (either a directed edge
`v₁ ⟵[G] v₂` or a bidirected edge `v₁ ⟷[G] v₂`). Definitionally
`hus G v₁ v₂` from def_3_2. -/
def EdgeInto (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := hus G v₁ v₂

/-- `EdgeOutOf G v₁ v₂` -- the edge between `v₁` and `v₂` is *out
of `v₁`*: a directed edge `v₁ ⟶[G] v₂`. Definitionally
`tuh G v₁ v₂` from def_3_2. Note the deliberate asymmetry with
`EdgeInto`: bidirected edges are excluded here, because a
bidirected edge has arrowheads at both endpoints and so is "into"
both vertices but "out of" neither. -/
def EdgeOutOf (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := tuh G v₁ v₂

/-- The LN's alternative spelling: "$v_2 \hut v_1$" also expresses
"out of $v_1$". Both `tuh G v₁ v₂` (canonical) and `hut G v₂ v₁`
(alternative) unfold to `(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E`, so the equivalence is
definitional. -/
theorem edgeOutOf_iff_hut (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) :
    EdgeOutOf G v₁ v₂ ↔ hut G v₂ v₁ := Iff.rfl

/-- Adjacency is symmetric. The directed-edge cases swap
`tuh`/`hut` (which are propositionally equal up to argument order);
the bidirected case uses `G.L_symm` from def_3_1. Later chapters
use this implicitly whenever they restate an "adjacent" hypothesis
with the arguments swapped. -/
theorem Adjacent.symm {G : CDMG α} {v₁ v₂ : α}
    (h : Adjacent G v₁ v₂) : Adjacent G v₂ v₁ := by
  rcases h with htuh | hhut | hhuh
  · exact Or.inr (Or.inl htuh)
  · exact Or.inl hhut
  · exact Or.inr (Or.inr (G.L_symm hhuh))
lean_code_without_comments:
def Adjacent (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := sus G v₁ v₂

def EdgeInto (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := hus G v₁ v₂

def EdgeOutOf (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) : Prop := tuh G v₁ v₂

theorem edgeOutOf_iff_hut (G : CDMG α) (v₁ v₂ : α) :
    EdgeOutOf G v₁ v₂ ↔ hut G v₂ v₁ := Iff.rfl

theorem Adjacent.symm {G : CDMG α} {v₁ v₂ : α}
    (h : Adjacent G v₁ v₂) : Adjacent G v₂ v₁ := by
  rcases h with htuh | hhut | hhuh
  · exact Or.inr (Or.inl htuh)
  · exact Or.inl hhut
  · exact Or.inr (Or.inr (G.L_symm hhuh))
lean_explanation:
This file lifts three of def 3.2's primitive edge relations to *named* aliases with prose-friendly identifiers, mirroring the lecture notes' editorial choice to give English names to three particular combinations of arrows.

The ambient binder is `α : Type*`, the carrier of vertices; this matches the LN's silent assumption that a graph is over some vertex set. Each definition takes a `G : CDMG α` (the bundle introduced in def 3.1) and a pair of vertices `v₁ v₂ : α`, and is `Prop`-valued.

`Adjacent G v₁ v₂` is defined as `sus G v₁ v₂`, the def 3.2 "some edge of any orientation" relation: a directed edge in either direction or a bidirected edge between the two vertices. `EdgeInto G v₁ v₂` is `hus G v₁ v₂`, the def 3.2 relation "there is an arrowhead at `v₁`" — either `v₁ ⟵[G] v₂` (a directed edge from `v₂` to `v₁`) or `v₁ ⟷[G] v₂` (a bidirected edge). `EdgeOutOf G v₁ v₂` is `tuh G v₁ v₂`, i.e. a *directed* edge `v₁ ⟶[G] v₂`; the bidirected case is deliberately absent. The LN's companion phrasing "edge into $v_2$" is the *same* `EdgeInto` predicate with the arguments swapped.

Because each definition is just an alias, the file also ships a `@[simp]` characterisation lemma for each name (`adjacent_iff`, `edgeInto_iff`, `edgeOutOf_iff`), each proved by `Iff.rfl`, so callers can rewrite freely between the prose name and its def 3.2 underlying form.

`edgeOutOf_iff_hut` records the LN's second spelling: "$v_2 \hut v_1$" also reads as "out of $v_1$". Both `tuh G v₁ v₂` and `hut G v₂ v₁` unfold to `(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E`, so the equivalence is `Iff.rfl`.

Finally, `Adjacent.symm` shows that the "adjacent" relation is symmetric. The proof `rcases` on the three disjuncts of `sus` (directed-out, directed-in, bidirected); the two directed cases swap by re-tagging the disjunction, and the bidirected case invokes `G.L_symm`, the symmetry field of the CDMG bundle from def 3.1.
design_choices:
**A `def`, not an `abbrev` or new notation.** The goal of this row is to introduce *readable Lean identifiers* for prose-level use, not new graph structure. An `abbrev` would dissolve into `sus` / `hus` / `tuh` on sight, defeating the purpose: downstream statements such as claim 3.1's "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" would render in goals and error messages as the def 3.2 primitive rather than as the LN's English name. A `notation` token would pile yet another arrow on top of the six already introduced in def 3.2, when what we want at this layer is precisely the English. A `def` is the right unit: it survives intact in goal output, it can be unfolded explicitly via the `@[simp]` `_iff` lemmas when a proof needs the underlying relation, and it keeps the def 3.2 arrows as the formal-syntax layer with these new identifiers as the prose-syntax layer on top.

**Convention: arrowhead at the *first* argument of `EdgeInto`.** Item 2 of the LN lists two sentences — "into $v_1$" and "into $v_2$" — that share the same predicate, differing only in which vertex argument receives the arrowhead. Rather than introducing two predicates, we follow the LN's natural wording and let `EdgeInto G v₁ v₂` mean "into `v₁`", with the arrowhead at the first listed vertex; the LN's "into $v_2$" sentence becomes `EdgeInto G v₂ v₁`. This convention also matches the underlying def 3.2 relation `hus`, which already picks out the first vertex as the arrowhead site, so no swapping is needed when unfolding.

**Bidirected edges deliberately excluded from `EdgeOutOf`.** A bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ has arrowheads at *both* endpoints, so it is "into" both vertices but "out of" neither. The LN encodes this asymmetry by listing $\huh$ only on the "into" side of item 2 and never on the "out of" side of item 3. This is load-bearing downstream: the directed-walk and ancestor relations introduced in later rows all condition on directed edges only, never bidirected ones, and baking that asymmetry into the prose names now keeps those later definitions clean.

**The LN's two spellings of "out of" collapse to one Lean proposition.** Item 3 offers both $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ as ways to express "out of $v_1$". In Lean these are literally the same proposition — both unfold to $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$ — so we pick `tuh G v₁ v₂` as the canonical form and record the alternative spelling as a separate `Iff.rfl` lemma `edgeOutOf_iff_hut`, giving readers who arrive via the $\hut$ side an immediate bridge to the canonical form.
